techniques.evidence_quote: A copy of the key was inadvertently posted by Uber on one of its public pages on the code development platform GitHub in March of 2014, prior to the breach.
An adversary discovered an Uber credential that was leaked in a GitHub Gist... to exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket.
The two sources said that the address from which the hack was launched is associated with a virtual private network service... known for vigorously protecting the privacy of its users.
The hazardous gist was discovered and found to be an Uber data analysis script containing AWS key material.
They succeeded in finding several AWS credentials and loaded them into a tool called S3 Browser. From here, the adversary discovered hourly encrypted backups.

pivoted to use a Scandinavian IP address behind a VPN to exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket on 2014-05-12. These S3 objects contained about 50,000 records, including names and driver's licenses.
A database prune redacts production data and generates datasets for local development... This pruning process was incomplete at removing sensitive data, putting customer data at risk.

attack_chain.description: The attacker discovered an Uber data analysis script (GitHub Gist) that had been inadvertently published publicly on GitHub in March 2014, containing AWS IAM key material.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: A copy of the key was inadvertently posted by Uber on one of its public pages on the code development platform GitHub in March of 2014, prior to the breach, the court filings show, and remained there for months.

attack_chain.description: The attacker harvested AWS credentials (IAM access key) from the publicly accessible GitHub Gist containing an Uber data analysis script.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The hazardous gist was discovered and found to be an Uber data analysis script containing AWS key material.

attack_chain.description: The attacker used the stolen AWS credentials to authenticate to the AWS API, accessing Uber's cloud environment without authorization.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: An adversary discovered an Uber credential that was leaked in a GitHub Gist... pivoted to use a Scandinavian IP address behind a VPN to exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket.

attack_chain.description: The attacker pivoted from a Comcast IP address (used to discover the credentials) to a Scandinavian VPN service to anonymize the actual exfiltration activity and avoid attribution.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Uber believes the adversary was on a Comcast IP address, which pivoted to use a Scandinavian IP address behind a VPN to exfiltrate several objects... The two sources said that the address from which the hack was launched is associated with a virtual private network service... known for vigorously protecting the privacy of its users.

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen AWS credentials and the S3 Browser tool, the attacker browsed Uber's S3 buckets to identify available objects, discovering database prune files stored in the cloud.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They succeeded in finding several AWS credentials and loaded them into a tool called S3 Browser. From here, the adversary discovered hourly encrypted backups.

attack_chain.description: The attacker exfiltrated approximately 50,000 records (driver names and license numbers) stored as database prune objects in Uber's S3 bucket on 2014-05-12.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: pivoted to use a Scandinavian IP address behind a VPN to exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket on 2014-05-12. These S3 objects contained about 50,000 records, including names and driver's licenses.

initial_access_vector: AWS IAM credentials inadvertently committed to a public GitHub Gist as part of a data analysis script, discovered and harvested by the attacker
persistence_mechanism: None identified in the sources; the attack appears to have been a one-time opportunistic exfiltration using the stolen static IAM key
privilege_escalation: None described; the stolen AWS credential had sufficient permissions to access the S3 bucket directly
